html:
SmartRent is a hated post-SPAC proptech name that the market still treats like a failed hardware rollout, while the business is becoming a small, cash-rich, hardware-enabled SaaS platform.

At $1.12, SMRT has a roughly $216 million market cap. The company ended Q1 2026 with $98.8 million of cash, no debt, and an undrawn $75 million credit facility, so the enterprise value is only about $116 million. Against Q1 ARR of $60.9 million, the market is paying under 2x ARR for a platform with 911,244 deployed units, roughly 600 customers, 15 of the top 20 multifamily operators, and near-zero churn in the core IoT product. That is the mispricing.

The surface story is weak. Q1 revenue fell 6% year over year, hardware units shipped fell 23%, bookings fell 32%, and ARR slipped sequentially from $61.6 million at year-end 2025 to $60.9 million. A screen says revenue is shrinking and the stock deserves to trade like a busted small-cap. That misses the transition. Core revenue, excluding non-cash hub amortization, was essentially flat year over year at $36.6 million. Gross margin expanded 630 bps to 39.1%. Operating expense fell 32%. Professional services moved from a $3.4 million gross loss to roughly breakeven. Adjusted EBITDA was positive for the second straight quarter.

The old SmartRent burned cash to seed apartments with devices. The new SmartRent is trying to monetize an installed base it already paid to build.

The key asset is not the hardware. It is the embedded operating layer across rental housing: locks, access control, thermostats, leak detection, package rooms, resident app, self-guided tours, work orders, inspections, audit management, and call automation. Once installed across a property, ripping it out is operationally disruptive. SmartRent’s 2025 customer churn for Smart Communities was only 0.15%, property net revenue retention was 108%, and customer net revenue retention was 110%. In Q1 2026, Smart Communities churn was 0.05%. The weak churn was in Smart Operations, not the core IoT layer, which management said has “virtually no churn.”

This distinction matters. If Smart Operations churn is a modular software issue, the stock is cheap. If core IoT churn rises, the thesis breaks. So far, the evidence supports the former.

The forward setup is better than the trailing financials. SmartRent has 911,000 deployed units and is pushing the “March to One Million” initiative. At the current Q1 SaaS ARPU of $5.63 per month, 1 million units alone implies roughly $67.6 million of ARR before meaningful renewal pricing, upsells, or new modules. Management also disclosed that about 300,000 early-adopter units are coming up for legacy contract renewals over time, with the first three renewals averaging a 33% pricing increase. That is high-quality incremental revenue because it mostly drops through the existing platform.

There is also a white-space angle the market is not underwriting. SmartRent’s existing customers own or control about 6.6 million U.S. units, meaning the company has penetrated only about 14% of its current customer footprint. This is not a speculative TAM slide based on unrelated customers. These are already relationships, already integrated into enterprise rental operators. If SmartRent merely grows inside that base, the ARR path can be far larger than today’s $61 million run rate.

The biggest recent strategic change is management quality. Frank Martell became CEO in June 2025 after running CoreLogic and loanDepot, and SmartRent has since turned from cost bloat toward operating leverage. Pankaj Bansi was promoted to COO in May 2026 after leading workflow and organizational redesign. This matters because SMRT does not need visionary reinvention. It needs disciplined execution: pricing renewals, sales productivity, installation margin control, inventory discipline, and avoiding low-return hardware volume. The Q1 print showed that work is underway.

The balance sheet gives the stock unusual optionality for a $1 microcap. Net cash is about $0.51 per share. There is also roughly $16.8 million remaining under the repurchase authorization. CEO insider buying in late 2025 at prices mostly above today’s quote adds a useful signal, though not a thesis by itself. Short interest is only about 1.6% of float, and StockTwits sentiment/message-volume data is basically inactive. This is not a crowded squeeze stock. It is more interesting than that: an ignored microcap where fundamental multiple expansion can do the work.

The base case does not require heroic growth. If ARR exits 2026 around $68 million to $72 million, adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow stay positive for the year, and the market gives the company 3.5x to 4.0x forward ARR, SMRT is worth about $1.90 to $2.10 per share after adding net cash. That multiple is still low for a sticky vertical SaaS platform, but fair for a company that still has hardware exposure and inconsistent bookings.

The bull case is that the market realizes the hardware collapse was the necessary clearing event, not the death of the business. Units deployed compound double digits through 2028, legacy renewals lift ARPU, the VAR channel opens the small and mid-market multifamily segment, hardware refresh becomes a recurring cadence, and ARR approaches $90 million to $100 million. At 4.5x to 5.0x ARR plus cash, the stock can reach $3.25 to $3.75. A strategic acquirer could justify that if it values SmartRent as distribution into enterprise multifamily rather than as a low-margin device seller.

The bear case is straightforward. Bookings weakness persists, Smart Operations churn spreads into core IoT, management spends the cash cushion chasing growth, and full-year free cash flow guidance proves too optimistic. If ARR stagnates around $60 million and the company returns to cash burn, the stock likely trades toward cash value minus restructuring and credibility discounts, around $0.60 to $0.70.

Current price: $1.12.

Bear target: $0.65, down 42%.

Base target: $2.00, up 79%.

Bull target: $3.50, up 213%.
